column3: Axial graphic shows a classic colloid cyst (CC) at the foramen of Monro causing mild/moderate obstructive hydrocephalus. Note that the fornices and choroid plexus are elevated and stretched over the cyst .
column5:
Colloid cyst

Caption-derived keyword findings:
foramen of Monro
mild/moderate obstructive hydrocephalus
fornices and choroid plexus elevated and stretched

Tight reasoning:
A round lesion at the foramen of Monro that elevates/drapes the fornices and produces obstructive lateral ventricular enlargement is the canonical anatomic pattern for a colloid cyst. Core also frames colloid cyst as the most common mass of the foramina of Monro.

Differential:
Subependymoma
Neurocysticercosis
Vertebrobasilar aneurysm
column21:
🧠 Colloid Cyst
Colloid cyst is a foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle cystic lesion that is classically recognized by a well-demarcated round hyperdense mass on noncontrast CT, with variable MR signal related to cyst contents. The most important board pivot is recognition before acute obstructive hydrocephalus.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer/STATdx synthesis. Core contributed the board-level intraventricular mass framing and classic imaging anchor; RadPrimer/STATdx contributed the expanded imaging spectrum, cases, differentials, pathology, risk, and management pivots.
📌 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • CC: colloid cyst.
  • NECT: noncontrast CT.
  • CCRS: Colloid Cyst Risk Score.
🔍 Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • Best diagnostic clue: well-demarcated round hyperdense foramen of Monro mass on NECT.
  • About two-thirds are hyperdense and about one-third are iso- or hypodense; CT density correlates inversely with hydration.
  • Hydrocephalus may be present; acute obstruction at the foramen of Monro can be fatal.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal correlates with cholesterol concentration: about two-thirds are T1 hyperintense and about one-third are isointense.
  • T2 signal is variable and reflects water content; many are isointense to brain, while mixed signal or black-hole hypointensity may reflect viscous/inspissated contents.
  • FLAIR signal does not suppress.
  • DWI should not show true restricted diffusion; ADC is often higher than brain and lower than CSF.
  • Peripheral/rim enhancement is rare and may occur in complicated inflammatory/apoplexy cases; displaced internal cerebral/septal veins can mimic rim enhancement.
🧬 Pathology / Mechanism
  • Colloid cysts arise from embryonic endoderm rather than neuroectoderm.
  • Cyst contents accumulate from mucinous secretions and desquamated epithelial cells; gross contents are thick and gelatinous with variable viscosity.
  • Histology shows a thin fibrous capsule with simple or pseudostratified epithelial lining, goblet cells, scattered ciliated cells, and PAS-positive gelatinous material.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Neurocysticercosis: usually multiple parenchymal/cisternal lesions, often with ependymitis or basilar meningitis, common calcification, and possible scolex.
  • CSF flow artifact/MR pseudocyst: confirm with multiplanar technique and look for phase artifact.
  • Vertebrobasilar dolichoectasia/aneurysm: look for flow void and phase artifact on MR.
  • Subependymoma: favors frontal horn or septum pellucidum attachment and patchy/solid enhancement.
  • Craniopharyngioma: rarely intraventricular, usually not wedged in the foramen of Monro, and more often calcifies or enhances.
🚩 Clinical / Risk Pivots
  • 40-50% are asymptomatic; headache occurs in about 50-60%.
  • Peak age is around 40 years; pediatric cases are rare but may deteriorate precipitously.
  • Account for about 0.5-1.0% of primary brain tumors and 15-20% of intraventricular masses.
  • CCRS uses age under 65 years, headache, cyst at least 7 mm, FLAIR hyperintensity, and anterior anatomic risk zone.
🛠️ Treatment / Reporting
  • Complete surgical resection is usual treatment; recurrence is rare after complete resection.
  • Hyperdensity on CT and T2 hypointensity suggest high viscosity and possible difficulty with stereotactic aspiration.
  • Referring clinicians should be notified immediately when a colloid cyst is identified, especially with hydrocephalus.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Think colloid cyst when a round, well-demarcated foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle lesion is hyperdense on CT, nonenhancing, FLAIR nonsuppressing, and variably T1/T2 intense.
  • The dangerous pivot is acute obstruction of the foramen of Monro causing rapid hydrocephalus despite sometimes small lesion size.

column3: Axial gross pathology in a patient who suddenly and inexplicably died shows a large CC causing moderate obstructive hydrocephalus. A small cavum septi pellucidi is present. Fornices are draped over the cyst. (Courtesy R. Hewlett, MD.)
column5:
Colloid cyst

Caption-derived keyword findings:
large CC
moderate obstructive hydrocephalus
fornices are draped over the cyst

Tight reasoning:
The gross pathology image directly localizes a large cystic lesion to the foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle region with obstructive hydrocephalus and fornix draping, matching the pathologic-anatomic pattern of colloid cyst.

Differential:

Xanthogranuloma

column3: Axial NECT in a 45-year-old man presenting with headaches demonstrates a classic CC, seen as a hyperdense mass wedged into the foramen of Monro and upper 3rd ventricle. Note that there is no dilatation of the lateral ventricles.
column5:

column3:
Axial T1 image from the same case.
Axial T1W MR in a 52-year-old man with headache, amnesia, and transient ischemic attack (TIA)-like symptoms shows a large, mildly hyperintense, well-delineated mass in the anterior 3rd ventricle at the foramen of Monro. Note the blood-fluid level in the dependent part of the mass.
Axial T2 image from the same case.
Axial T2 MR in the same patient shows the mass is cystic and even more hyperintense than CSF in the adjacent ventricles. Note the blood-fluid level and splaying of the fornices around the mass.
Thin-section T2-space image from the same case.
Axial thin-section T2-space MR shows the fornices draped around the mass.
Axial FLAIR image from the same case.
Axial FLAIR MR shows the cyst is extremely hyperintense and does not suppress. Note hyperintensity in the fornices and adjacent corpus callosum genu suggesting edema from acute inflammation.
Axial postcontrast image from the same case.
Axial T1 C+ FS MR shows rim enhancement around the cyst wall.
Coronal postcontrast image from the same case.
Coronal T1 C+ MR in the same patient shows the rim enhancement surrounds the entire cyst wall. Diagnosis of a CC with apoplexy, intracystic hemorrhage, and inflammatory changes was documented at surgery.
column5:
Colloid cyst

Caption-derived keyword findings:
anterior 3rd ventricle at the foramen of Monro
blood-fluid level
splaying of the fornices
does not suppress
rim enhancement around the cyst wall

Tight reasoning:
The multi-sequence cluster localizes a cystic mass to the anterior third ventricle/foramen of Monro, shows blood-fluid level/apoplexy features, fornix splaying/draping, FLAIR nonsuppression, and rare rim enhancement. These source-stated features support complicated colloid cyst with apoplexy/intracystic hemorrhage and inflammatory change.

Differential:
Neurocysticercosis
Craniopharyngioma
Subependymoma
Xanthogranuloma

column3: Axial DWI image shows no restricted diffusion . Although CCs usually have viscid gelatinous material, they do not show restricted diffusion. ADC values are often higher than that of normal cerebral parenchyma and lower than CSF.
column5:
Colloid cyst

Caption-derived keyword findings:
no restricted diffusion
viscid gelatinous material
ADC values are often higher than normal cerebral parenchyma and lower than CSF

Tight reasoning:
Colloid cysts can contain viscid gelatinous material but should not show true restricted diffusion. This helps keep colloid cyst separate from diffusion-bright mimics such as epidermoid-type cystic lesions when the lesion is in the expected foramen of Monro region.

Differential:
Epidermoid cyst
Neurocysticercosis
Xanthogranuloma

column21:
🧠 Colloid Cyst
Colloid cyst is a foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle cystic lesion that is classically recognized by a well-demarcated round hyperdense mass on noncontrast CT, with variable MR signal related to cyst contents. The most important board pivot is recognition before acute obstructive hydrocephalus.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer/STATdx synthesis. Core contributed the board-level intraventricular mass framing and classic imaging anchor; RadPrimer/STATdx contributed the expanded imaging spectrum, cases, differentials, pathology, risk, and management pivots.
📌 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • CC: colloid cyst.
  • NECT: noncontrast CT.
  • CCRS: Colloid Cyst Risk Score.
🔍 Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • Best diagnostic clue: well-demarcated round hyperdense foramen of Monro mass on NECT.
  • About two-thirds are hyperdense and about one-third are iso- or hypodense; CT density correlates inversely with hydration.
  • Hydrocephalus may be present; acute obstruction at the foramen of Monro can be fatal.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal correlates with cholesterol concentration: about two-thirds are T1 hyperintense and about one-third are isointense.
  • T2 signal is variable and reflects water content; many are isointense to brain, while mixed signal or black-hole hypointensity may reflect viscous/inspissated contents.
  • FLAIR signal does not suppress.
  • DWI should not show true restricted diffusion; ADC is often higher than brain and lower than CSF.
  • Peripheral/rim enhancement is rare and may occur in complicated inflammatory/apoplexy cases; displaced internal cerebral/septal veins can mimic rim enhancement.
🧬 Pathology / Mechanism
  • Colloid cysts arise from embryonic endoderm rather than neuroectoderm.
  • Cyst contents accumulate from mucinous secretions and desquamated epithelial cells; gross contents are thick and gelatinous with variable viscosity.
  • Histology shows a thin fibrous capsule with simple or pseudostratified epithelial lining, goblet cells, scattered ciliated cells, and PAS-positive gelatinous material.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Neurocysticercosis: usually multiple parenchymal/cisternal lesions, often with ependymitis or basilar meningitis, common calcification, and possible scolex.
  • CSF flow artifact/MR pseudocyst: confirm with multiplanar technique and look for phase artifact.
  • Vertebrobasilar dolichoectasia/aneurysm: look for flow void and phase artifact on MR.
  • Subependymoma: favors frontal horn or septum pellucidum attachment and patchy/solid enhancement.
  • Craniopharyngioma: rarely intraventricular, usually not wedged in the foramen of Monro, and more often calcifies or enhances.
🚩 Clinical / Risk Pivots
  • 40-50% are asymptomatic; headache occurs in about 50-60%.
  • Peak age is around 40 years; pediatric cases are rare but may deteriorate precipitously.
  • Account for about 0.5-1.0% of primary brain tumors and 15-20% of intraventricular masses.
  • CCRS uses age under 65 years, headache, cyst at least 7 mm, FLAIR hyperintensity, and anterior anatomic risk zone.
🛠️ Treatment / Reporting
  • Complete surgical resection is usual treatment; recurrence is rare after complete resection.
  • Hyperdensity on CT and T2 hypointensity suggest high viscosity and possible difficulty with stereotactic aspiration.
  • Referring clinicians should be notified immediately when a colloid cyst is identified, especially with hydrocephalus.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Think colloid cyst when a round, well-demarcated foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle lesion is hyperdense on CT, nonenhancing, FLAIR nonsuppressing, and variably T1/T2 intense.
  • The dangerous pivot is acute obstruction of the foramen of Monro causing rapid hydrocephalus despite sometimes small lesion size.

column3:
Axial noncontrast CT image from the same case.
Axial NECT in a 24-year-old man with severe headaches shows a hyperdense mass at the foramen of Monro. The superficial sulci are effaced, and the brain appears "tight," suggesting moderate cerebral edema.
Axial T1 image from the same case.
Axial T1 MR in the same patient shows the mass is slightly hyperintense compared to the brain.
Axial T2 image from the same case.
Axial T2 MR in the same patient shows most of the cyst appears relatively isointense with gray matter but exhibits a central hypointense focus that suggests inspissated colloid within the cyst.
Coronal T2 image from the same case.
Coronal T2 MR shows the central inspissated cyst as very hypointense. Note elevation and splaying of the fornices around the cyst.
Stereotactic postcontrast image from the same case.
Axial T1 C+ SPGR MR for stereotaxic surgery shows enhancement around the lateral margins of the cyst. This represents displaced internal cerebral and septal veins, not rim enhancement. An inspissated CC was removed at surgery.

column9:
Pattern differs because this drill trains a hyperdense foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle mass pattern on CT.
Colloid cyst -> round, well-demarcated foramen of Monro/anterior roof third ventricle lesion; typically nonenhancing and often hyperattenuating on CT.

Vertebrobasilar aneurysm/dolichoectasia -> vascular mimic; look for flow void and phase artifact on MR or confirm with vascular imaging.

column15: Define colloid cyst.
column16:
Source definition: Colloid cyst is a unilocular mucin-containing cyst of the 3rd ventricle, also called a paraphyseal or endodermal cyst.
Refined operational definition based on the allowed source set: a benign developmental, usually nonenhancing cyst characteristically arising from the anterior roof of the 3rd ventricle/foramen of Monro.
Boundary: it is an endodermal cystic lesion, not a neuroectodermal tumor; it is usually wedged at the foramen of Monro rather than broadly attached to the septum/frontal horn.
Synonyms: paraphyseal cyst; endodermal cyst.

column11: Why can a colloid cyst cause abrupt obstructive hydrocephalus?
column12: Normal CSF exits the lateral ventricles through the foramina of Monro into the 3rd ventricle. Colloid cysts are characteristically wedged into this narrow foramen/anterior-superior roof of the 3rd ventricle, where the fornices can drape around the cyst. When the cyst blocks one or both foramina of Monro, CSF outflow from the lateral ventricles is obstructed, producing rapid lateral ventricular enlargement, transependymal CSF flow/interstitial edema, herniation, coma, or death. Imaging pivot: a foramen of Monro mass plus lateral ventricular dilatation is not just a location clue; it is the mechanism of the emergency.

column11: What tissue/content mechanism explains the variable CT and MR appearance of colloid cysts?
column12: Colloid cysts arise from embryonic endodermal elements and have a thin fibrous capsule with epithelial lining that includes goblet and scattered ciliated cells. The cyst accumulates mucinous secretions and desquamated epithelial cells, creating thick gelatinous material with variable viscosity, hydration, necrotic debris, cholesterol, and sometimes old blood. Imaging pivot: CT density correlates inversely with hydration; T1 signal correlates with cholesterol concentration; T2 signal reflects water content, while viscous/inspissated contents can appear markedly T2 hypointense or produce a black-hole effect.

  • NECT: noncontrast CT.
  • CCRS: Colloid Cyst Risk Score.
🔍 Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • Best diagnostic clue: well-demarcated round hyperdense foramen of Monro mass on NECT.
  • About two-thirds are hyperdense and about one-third are iso- or hypodense; CT density correlates inversely with hydration.
  • Hydrocephalus may be present; acute obstruction at the foramen of Monro can be fatal.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal correlates with cholesterol concentration: about two-thirds are T1 hyperintense and about one-third are isointense.
  • T2 signal is variable and reflects water content; many are isointense to brain, while mixed signal or black-hole hypointensity may reflect viscous/inspissated contents.
  • FLAIR signal does not suppress.
  • DWI should not show true restricted diffusion; ADC is often higher than brain and lower than CSF.
  • Peripheral/rim enhancement is rare and may occur in complicated inflammatory/apoplexy cases; displaced internal cerebral/septal veins can mimic rim enhancement.

column3: Axial T1 C+ SPGR MR for stereotaxic surgery shows enhancement around the lateral margins of the cyst. This represents displaced internal cerebral and septal veins, not rim enhancement. An inspissated CC was removed at surgery.
column13: What visual pitfall can occur on this stereotactic MR image, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Mistaking enhancement along the cyst margins for true cyst-wall rim enhancement.
Safety check: Trace the enhancing margins anatomically as displaced internal cerebral and septal veins when they course along the lateral cyst margins.
Key discriminator: true rim enhancement surrounds the cyst wall; in this case the source states the enhancement represents displaced veins, not rim enhancement.
Why it happens: venous structures displaced around a foramen of Monro cyst can visually simulate a thin enhancing cyst wall.

column13: What is the danger in dismissing a small or incidental colloid cyst?
column14:
Trap: Assuming small size or incidental discovery eliminates clinical risk.
Safety check: Always assess for hydrocephalus and communicate the diagnosis, especially if ventricular obstruction is present.
Key discriminator: the risk is anatomic obstruction at the foramen of Monro, not simply lesion size.
Why it happens: colloid cysts may be stable or incidental, but acute foramen of Monro obstruction can cause rapid hydrocephalus, herniation, coma, or death, including with small lesions.

column15: What is the typical age distribution for colloid cyst?
column16:

column15: For colloid cyst, what is the classic CT appearance?
column16:
Noncontrast CT: well-demarcated round hyperdense mass at the foramen of Monro/anterior upper 3rd ventricle.

column15: For colloid cyst, what location pattern is most source-supported?
column16:
Classic location: foramen of Monro/anterior-superior roof of the 3rd ventricle.
Frequency: more than 99% are wedged into the foramen of Monro.
Size: ranges from a few millimeters to several centimeters, with mean around 15 mm.
Exam pivot: a well-demarcated lesion at this narrow CSF outflow point is both a diagnosis clue and an obstruction risk.

The most important board pivot is recognition before acute obstructive hydrocephalus.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer/STATdx synthesis. Core contributed the board-level intraventricular mass framing and classic imaging anchor; RadPrimer/STATdx contributed the expanded imaging spectrum, cases, differentials, pathology, risk, and management pivots.
📌 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • CC: colloid cyst.
  • NECT: noncontrast CT.
  • CCRS: Colloid Cyst Risk Score.
🔍 Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • Best diagnostic clue: well-demarcated round hyperdense foramen of Monro mass on NECT.
  • About two-thirds are hyperdense and about one-third are iso- or hypodense; CT density correlates inversely with hydration.
  • Hydrocephalus may be present; acute obstruction at the foramen of Monro can be fatal.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal correlates with cholesterol concentration: about two-thirds are T1 hyperintense and about one-third are isointense.
  • T2 signal is variable and reflects water content; many are isointense to brain, while mixed signal or black-hole hypointensity may reflect viscous/inspissated contents.
  • FLAIR signal does not suppress.
  • DWI should not show true restricted diffusion; ADC is often higher than brain and lower than CSF.
  • Peripheral/rim enhancement is rare and may occur in complicated inflammatory/apoplexy cases; displaced internal cerebral/septal veins can mimic rim enhancement.
🧬 Pathology / Mechanism
  • Colloid cysts arise from embryonic endoderm rather than neuroectoderm.
  • Cyst contents accumulate from mucinous secretions and desquamated epithelial cells; gross contents are thick and gelatinous with variable viscosity.
  • Histology shows a thin fibrous capsule with simple or pseudostratified epithelial lining, goblet cells, scattered ciliated cells, and PAS-positive gelatinous material.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Neurocysticercosis: usually multiple parenchymal/cisternal lesions, often with ependymitis or basilar meningitis, common calcification, and possible scolex.
  • CSF flow artifact/MR pseudocyst: confirm with multiplanar technique and look for phase artifact.
  • Vertebrobasilar dolichoectasia/aneurysm: look for flow void and phase artifact on MR.
  • Subependymoma: favors frontal horn or septum pellucidum attachment and patchy/solid enhancement.
  • Craniopharyngioma: rarely intraventricular, usually not wedged in the foramen of Monro, and more often calcifies or enhances.
🚩 Clinical / Risk Pivots
  • 40-50% are asymptomatic; headache occurs in about 50-60%.
  • Peak age is around 40 years; pediatric cases are rare but may deteriorate precipitously.
  • Account for about 0.5-1.0% of primary brain tumors and 15-20% of intraventricular masses.
  • CCRS uses age under 65 years, headache, cyst at least 7 mm, FLAIR hyperintensity, and anterior anatomic risk zone.
🛠️ Treatment / Reporting
  • Complete surgical resection is usual treatment; recurrence is rare after complete resection.
  • Hyperdensity on CT and T2 hypointensity suggest high viscosity and possible difficulty with stereotactic aspiration.
  • Referring clinicians should be notified immediately when a colloid cyst is identified, especially with hydrocephalus.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Think colloid cyst when a round, well-demarcated foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle lesion is hyperdense on CT, nonenhancing, FLAIR nonsuppressing, and variably T1/T2 intense.
  • The dangerous pivot is acute obstruction of the foramen of Monro causing rapid hydrocephalus despite sometimes small lesion size.

column15: For colloid cyst, what T1 MRI signal pattern is source-supported?
column16:
T1 signal correlates with cholesterol concentration.
About two-thirds are T1 hyperintense and about one-third are isointense.
Exam pivot: a T1-hyperintense anterior third ventricular/foramen of Monro lesion strongly supports colloid cyst, but isointense cysts still occur and may be harder to see.

column15: For colloid cyst, what T2 signal pattern is source-supported?
column16:
T2 signal is variable and generally reflects water content.
Many colloid cysts are isointense to brain.
About 25% show mixed hypo/hyperintensity or a black-hole/profound hypointense component from viscous or inspissated contents.
Exam pivot: T2 hypointensity is not a reason to discard colloid cyst; it can be the clue to thick contents.

column15: For colloid cyst, what FLAIR and DWI behavior is source-supported?
column16:
FLAIR: colloid cyst signal does not suppress.
DWI: should not show true restricted diffusion despite viscous gelatinous contents.
ADC: often higher than normal brain and lower than CSF.
Exam pivot: nonrestriction helps avoid mistaking a viscous colloid cyst for a diffusion-restricting intraventricular process.

column15: For colloid cyst, what enhancement and apoplexy caveats are source-supported?
column16:
Usual enhancement: absent/nonenhancing.
Rare caveat: peripheral/rim enhancement can occur in complicated inflammatory or apoplexy cases.
Apoplexy clue: fluid-fluid or blood-fluid levels are rare and indicate cyst apoplexy/hemorrhage.
Exam pivot: do not let rare rim enhancement or hemorrhagic/apoplectic change erase the foramen of Monro diagnosis.

column15: Which source-supported features favor colloid cyst stability?
column16:
Features favoring stability: older age, small cyst size, no hydrocephalus, hyperdensity on CT, and T2 hypointensity.
Natural history pivot: most cysts remain stable or stop enlarging; approximately 10% enlarge.
Exam pivot: hyperdense CT + T2 hypointensity suggests inspissated/less hydrated contents and tends to favor stability in the source framework.

column15: What reporting action is emphasized when a colloid cyst is identified?
column16:
Notify the referring clinician immediately, especially if hydrocephalus is present.

column15: What treatment/observation framework is source-supported for colloid cyst?
column16:
Usual treatment: complete surgical resection, commonly neuronavigation-guided endoscopic removal with capsule coagulation.
After complete resection: recurrence is rare; short-term memory disturbance may occur after surgery and usually resolves.
Observation: uncommon and generally not recommended because sudden obstruction can occur even with small cysts; serial imaging may be appropriate for asymptomatic cysts under 1 cm without hydrocephalus.

column15: At the foramen of Monro, how does age shift the leading diagnosis?
column16:
Adults: colloid cyst is the most common mass of the foramen of Monro.
Children: SEGA is the key competing diagnosis.
Source basis: Core evidence explicitly supports this adult-versus-child framing.

column15: How does subependymoma differ from colloid cyst as a foramen of Monro/anterior ventricular mimic?
column16:
Colloid cyst: round, well-demarcated lesion wedged at the foramen of Monro/anterior roof of the 3rd ventricle, usually nonenhancing.
Subependymoma: favors frontal horn or septum pellucidum attachment and patchy/solid enhancement.
Exam pivot: attachment site and enhancement pattern are the discriminator.

column15: How does neurocysticercosis differ from colloid cyst as an intraventricular/foramen of Monro mimic?
column16:
Colloid cyst: solitary foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricular lesion with classic CT hyperdensity and variable MR signal.
Neurocysticercosis: usually multiple; calcification or scolex may be present, often with ependymitis or basilar meningitis.
Exam pivot: multiplicity, calcification/scolex, and inflammatory meningitis/ependymitis favor neurocysticercosis.

column15: How can vertebrobasilar dolichoectasia or aneurysm mimic colloid cyst, and what separates it?
column16:
Mimic pattern: a vascular structure can simulate a hyperdense foramen of Monro mass.
Discriminator: look for flow void and phase artifact on MR, or confirm with vascular imaging.
Exam pivot: do not call a vascular mimic a cyst before checking vascular signal/artifact.

column15: Which additional intraventricular mimics are source-supported for colloid cyst, and what is the key caveat?
column16:
Craniopharyngioma: rarely involves the 3rd ventricle, is usually not wedged into the foramen of Monro, and more often calcifies or shows rim/nodular enhancement.
Choroid plexus papilloma, xanthogranuloma, and choroid plexus cyst are additional intraventricular mimics.
Key caveat: xanthogranuloma may be indistinguishable on imaging.

Colloid cyst is a foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle cystic lesion that is classically recognized by a well-demarcated round hyperdense mass on noncontrast CT, with variable MR signal related to cyst contents. The most important board pivot is recognition before acute obstructive hydrocephalus.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer/STATdx synthesis. Core contributed the board-level intraventricular mass framing and classic imaging anchor; RadPrimer/STATdx contributed the expanded imaging spectrum, cases, differentials, pathology, risk, and management pivots.
📌 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • CC: colloid cyst.
  • NECT: noncontrast CT.
  • CCRS: Colloid Cyst Risk Score.
🔍 Key Imaging Findings
CT
  • Best diagnostic clue: well-demarcated round hyperdense foramen of Monro mass on NECT.
  • About two-thirds are hyperdense and about one-third are iso- or hypodense; CT density correlates inversely with hydration.
  • Hydrocephalus may be present; acute obstruction at the foramen of Monro can be fatal.
MR Imaging
  • T1 signal correlates with cholesterol concentration: about two-thirds are T1 hyperintense and about one-third are isointense.
  • T2 signal is variable and reflects water content; many are isointense to brain, while mixed signal or black-hole hypointensity may reflect viscous/inspissated contents.
  • FLAIR signal does not suppress.
  • DWI should not show true restricted diffusion; ADC is often higher than brain and lower than CSF.
  • Peripheral/rim enhancement is rare and may occur in complicated inflammatory/apoplexy cases; displaced internal cerebral/septal veins can mimic rim enhancement.
🧬 Pathology / Mechanism
  • Colloid cysts arise from embryonic endoderm rather than neuroectoderm.
  • Cyst contents accumulate from mucinous secretions and desquamated epithelial cells; gross contents are thick and gelatinous with variable viscosity.
  • Histology shows a thin fibrous capsule with simple or pseudostratified epithelial lining, goblet cells, scattered ciliated cells, and PAS-positive gelatinous material.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Neurocysticercosis: usually multiple parenchymal/cisternal lesions, often with ependymitis or basilar meningitis, common calcification, and possible scolex.
  • CSF flow artifact/MR pseudocyst: confirm with multiplanar technique and look for phase artifact.
  • Vertebrobasilar dolichoectasia/aneurysm: look for flow void and phase artifact on MR.
  • Subependymoma: favors frontal horn or septum pellucidum attachment and patchy/solid enhancement.
  • Craniopharyngioma: rarely intraventricular, usually not wedged in the foramen of Monro, and more often calcifies or enhances.
🚩 Clinical / Risk Pivots
  • 40-50% are asymptomatic; headache occurs in about 50-60%.
  • Peak age is around 40 years; pediatric cases are rare but may deteriorate precipitously.
  • Account for about 0.5-1.0% of primary brain tumors and 15-20% of intraventricular masses.
  • CCRS uses age under 65 years, headache, cyst at least 7 mm, FLAIR hyperintensity, and anterior anatomic risk zone.
🛠️ Treatment / Reporting
  • Complete surgical resection is usual treatment; recurrence is rare after complete resection.
  • Hyperdensity on CT and T2 hypointensity suggest high viscosity and possible difficulty with stereotactic aspiration.
  • Referring clinicians should be notified immediately when a colloid cyst is identified, especially with hydrocephalus.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Think colloid cyst when a round, well-demarcated foramen of Monro/anterior third ventricle lesion is hyperdense on CT, nonenhancing, FLAIR nonsuppressing, and variably T1/T2 intense.
  • The dangerous pivot is acute obstruction of the foramen of Monro causing rapid hydrocephalus despite sometimes small lesion size.
